mnemonics.title: Init Is Initialisation
mnemonics.content: __init__ stands for 'initialize' - it's the constructor that sets up your new object when you use ClassName()

mnemonics.title: Self Is The Object Itself
mnemonics.content: Always remember: self represents the current instance of the class - think 'I am referring to myself'

mnemonics.title: Dunder Del Is Destruction
mnemonics.content: __del__ is the destructor - called when an object is about to be destroyed (DUNDER = Double UNDERscore)

mnemonics.title: Class Methods Get CLS
mnemonics.content: @classmethod methods receive 'cls' (class) as first parameter, @staticmethod methods receive neither self nor cls

mnemonics.title: Constructor Always First
mnemonics.content: __init__ is always the first method to run when creating an object - it 'constructs' the object before anything else

mnemonics.title: Methods Need Self
mnemonics.content: Instance methods must have 'self' as first parameter - without self, you can't access instance variables

acronyms.usage: Constructor method - automatically called when object is created. Used to initialize instance variables.
Destructor method - called when object is about to be garbage collected. Use sparingly.
First parameter in instance methods, refers to the current object instance being operated on.
First parameter in @classmethod methods, refers to the class itself (not the instance).
Paradigm using classes and objects - constructors and methods are core OOP concepts in Python.

visualTips.visualization: Picture a factory assembly line - __init__ is the machine that sets up/initializes each new product (object) as it comes off the line
Imagine self as a mirror - when a method says 'self.name', it's like looking in a mirror and saying 'my name'
Think of each object as a person - instance methods are actions that one specific person can do (each object has its own copy)
Picture a government office - class methods affect the entire system/class, not individual citizens (instances)
Imagine a toolbox - static methods are like standalone tools that don't need a specific project (object) to work
Each object is a unique house - instance variables are the furniture inside each house, unique to that house

analogies.analogy: Like a blueprint being used to build a house - the constructor sets up all the rooms (variables) before the house (object) is ready to live in
Like using 'I' when speaking about yourself - self is how an object refers to its own data and methods
Like a person having abilities (walking, talking) - each person (object) has their own copy of these abilities
Like school rules that apply to all students - class methods affect the whole class, not just one student
Like a calculator that anyone can use without being a specific person - it doesn't need 'self' or 'cls' to work
Like a demolition crew that comes when a building is being torn down - __del__ cleans up before the object is destroyed
